planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light with very high humidity (70–80%); Anthurium magnificum is strictly a tropical collector's plant in zone 9 and requires consistently warm temperatures (65–85°F) and the humid conditions of its native Colombian cloud forest habitat. Use a chunky, well-aerated epiphytic mix and water thoroughly when the top layer dries. The leaves are very large and prone to mechanical damage — handle carefully. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Anthurium magnificum produces its most spectacular foliage in conditions of consistent bright filtered light, ideally from a bright north or east window, and will decline in dim corners. Anthurium magnificum is a large, terrestrial to lithophytic anthurium native to the cloud forests of Colombia, closely related to Anthurium crystallinum and belonging to the same 'velvet anthurium' group. The species name 'magnificum' is apt: the mature plant produces truly magnificent large leaves — broadly heart-shaped, up to 24–30 inches across, with a thickly leathery, velvety texture, very dark emerald-green surface, and a bold network of raised, pale ivory to cream veins that create an intricate pattern across the dark leaf face. The leaves are quadrangular-cordate (squarish heart shape) with distinctly angled basal lobes, a feature that distinguishes it from the more rounded lobes of A. crystallinum. The combination of large size, cardboard-thick leaf texture, dramatic vein pattern, and rich coloration makes A. magnificum one of the most impressive velvet anthuriums available. The species' considerable variation in the wild has led to complex classification and collector debate over 'true magnificum.' All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium magnificum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium collector species for indoor culture
American Orchid Society — Velvet Anthurium cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light with high humidity (60–80%) and very excellent drainage; Anthurium veitchii is strictly a tropical collector's plant in zone 9, requiring consistently warm temperatures and cloud-forest humidity conditions. Mount on a bark slab like an orchid for the most natural and healthy growth — the pendant leaves hang dramatically from an elevated position. Do not let the plant sit in water. New leaves emerge vertically and slowly unfurl; avoid disturbing them during this process. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on bark or in a hanging basket in bright indirect to moderate shade — King Anthurium's pendant leaves require vertical space to hang freely and display their full ornamental effect. Anthurium veitchii is a spectacular epiphytic anthurium from the cloud forests of Colombia, named for John Gould Veitch, the Victorian-era plant collector and nurseryman who introduced many South American aroids to cultivation. The species is distinguished from all other commonly grown anthuriums by its extraordinary leaves: enormously elongated, strap-shaped or tongue-shaped pendant leaves that in mature specimens can reach 4–6 feet in length, deeply and regularly corrugated (quilted) with a parallel rib pattern, rich bright emerald-green, and hanging gracefully downward from the plant's center — giving the plant the unmistakable presence of a large, dangling exotic that has earned it the title 'King Anthurium.' Young plants produce smaller, more erect leaves that gradually elongate and develop the characteristic corrugated texture and pendant habit as the plant matures. The flowers are the typical Anthurium green-white spathe, inconsequential compared to the dramatic foliage. Anthurium veitchii is among the most valued and sought-after collector aroids worldwide precisely because well-grown mature specimens with fully-developed pendant leaves are extraordinarily beautiful and genuinely rare in cultivation. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium veitchii species notes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Anthurium veitchii: King Anthurium profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium epiphytes for collectors
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Bird's Nest Anthurium (Hookeri Anthurium, Lance-Leaf Bird's Nest)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant, shaded patio container, or in zone 10-11 outdoor shade gardens; Anthurium hookeri is more tolerant of lower humidity, lower light, and temperature fluctuation than most collector anthuriums, making it a good choice for beginners in the genus. In zone 9b-10 in deep shade, it can be grown as an outdoor tropical groundcover or specimen. Provide moist, well-drained, humus-rich growing medium. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in moderate to deep shade in consistently moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or potting medium; Bird's Nest Anthurium is one of the most adaptable and shade-tolerant of the large Anthurium species, tolerating the reduced light and humidity of typical indoor environments better than most of its relatives. Anthurium hookeri is a large epiphytic to terrestrial anthurium from the forests of Trinidad, the Lesser Antilles, Guyana, and northeastern South America, growing in a distinctive bird's nest rosette form — the large, broadly elongated, glossy, dark-green leaves (12–24 inches long) radiate outward in a vase or funnel shape similar to Asplenium nidus, the Bird's Nest Fern, and for the same functional reason: the funnel shape channels rain and falling debris toward the plant's root zone, efficiently gathering nutrients and moisture. The leaves are thick, leathery, glossy, and a rich dark emerald-green with deeply impressed veins. Unlike the demanding velvet anthuriums, A. hookeri tolerates moderate indoor conditions, lower humidity, and even some neglect while remaining attractive. It produces typical anthurium flowers (green-white spathe and spadix) that, when pollinated, develop into small, purple, globose berries along the spadix. An excellent large specimen foliage plant for zone 9 shaded indoor and outdoor spaces. All parts toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium hookeri for Florida shade gardens
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Anthurium hookeri
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium hookeri species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Tricolor Chinese Evergreen (Camouflage Plant, Aglaonema pictum)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant in bright indirect to low light; Aglaonema pictum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Unlike the common Chinese Evergreen cultivars, A. pictum is more demanding — it prefers higher humidity (above 50%) and more consistent warmth than typical indoor conditions. Use a well-draining potting mix with added perlite. Water when the top inch dries. The camouflage leaf pattern is most vivid in consistent moderate indirect light. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to low light — Aglaonema pictum is one of the most shade-tolerant of the ornamental foliage plants and one of the very few popular houseplants that can maintain its decorative appearance in genuinely low light conditions, though growth slows considerably. Aglaonema pictum is a species of Chinese Evergreen from the tropical forests of Sumatra and nearby islands in Indonesia, producing one of the most immediately arresting leaf patterns in the houseplant world. The large, elongated, matte, dark-green leaves are decorated with irregular patches and blotches of medium sage-green and silvery pale green-white in a distribution pattern so precisely resembling military camouflage fabric that the plant has become universally known as the 'Camouflage Plant.' The three-toned pattern — dark base, mid-green mottling, bright silver-white patches — is produced by irregular distribution of chloroplasts and other pigments, and the pattern is never repeated identically across any two leaves, creating a naturally varied ornamental effect. Aglaonema pictum is considerably more demanding than the common hybrid Chinese Evergreens (Aglaonema commutatum varieties) typically seen in commercial settings — it requires higher humidity and more consistent warmth — but its unique camouflage pattern has made it a coveted collector plant among houseplant enthusiasts. Multiple 'tricolor' forms exist with varying intensities of the three-color pattern. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Aglaonema pictum cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aglaonema species for indoor tropical culture
American Horticulture Society — Aglaonema pictum: camouflage pattern
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Aglaonema (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Start tubers indoors in shallow trays of moist peat or potting mix with the concave side up in late winter; Florist's Gloxinia is typically sold as potted flowering tubers in bloom and grown as a houseplant. After flowering, allow the plant to go dormant by reducing watering — the foliage will yellow and die back. Store dry tubers in peat moss at 55–60°F through winter, then restart in fresh potting mix in late winter. Seeds are extremely fine — do not cover, just surface-sow on moist mix under fluorescent lights. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Florist's Gloxinia flowers most prolifically in the bright indirect light of a north or east window and will refuse to bloom in dim conditions; direct sun scorches the large, velvety leaves. Sinningia speciosa is a tuberous gesneriad from the rainforests of southern Brazil, cultivated since the 1820s and now available in an extraordinary range of cultivars and hybrids that have transformed the originally modest native plant into one of the most spectacular of all indoor-blooming plants. The flower buds emerge directly from the center of the plant and open to large, flared, trumpet-shaped blooms 3–4 inches across in vivid colors including violet, purple, white, pink, red, and bicolored forms with spotted, frilled, or picotee edges — all with a velvety texture matching the large, soft, dark-green, hairy leaves that surround them. The combination of huge, velvety flowers in saturated colors and the compact rosette of lush foliage makes a well-grown Gloxinia in full bloom one of the most visually striking of all flowering houseplants. Modern hybrid varieties bloom more freely and over longer periods than the species, and can be regrown from tubers through multiple seasons. The name 'Gloxinia' commonly used in commerce is technically incorrect — true Gloxinia is a related but distinct genus; the florist plant is correctly Sinningia speciosa. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Gesneriad Society — Sinningia speciosa (Florist's Gloxinia) care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Sinningia speciosa cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gesneriads for indoor flowering in Florida
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Sinningia speciosa (non-toxic to pets)
